conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 9 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you some questions.

First, are you responsible for Alex? This means you live with him and are responsible for his day-to-day care (such as feeding, clothing, and providing for his needs).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex doesn't live with me at the moment, but I do help support him financially. I contribute towards his upkeep regularly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. 

If Alex doesn't live with you, does he live with someone else who is responsible for his day-to-day care (such as another parent, guardian, or family member)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives with his other parent who looks after him day-to-day.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Just to confirm - do you live with Alex or are you the person primarily responsible for his day-to-day care (such as feeding him, clothing him, and making decisions about his daily needs)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not the one responsible for his day-to-day care. His other parent handles all of that - I don't live with him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. I have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Result:**

I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Reason:**

To claim Child Benefit, you must be "responsible" for the child. This means you need to:
- Live with the child, AND
- Be responsible for their day-to-day care (such as feeding, clothing, and providing for their needs)

While you do contribute financially to Alex's upkeep, which is commendable, you don't live with him and you're not responsible for his day-to-day care. Since Alex lives with his other parent who handles all of his daily care, they would be the person eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time, and this is typically the person the child lives with who provides their day-to-day care.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 9 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: To be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, you must be responsible for the child. Being 'responsible' means you must live with the child and be responsible for their day-to-day care (such as feeding, clothing, and providing for their needs). While you contribute financially to Alex's upkeep, you do not live with him and are not responsible for his day-to-day care. Alex lives with his other parent who handles all of his day-to-day care. Therefore, you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Child Benefit. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and this would typically be the person Alex lives with who provides his day-to-day care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 9), but does not live with him or provide day-to-day care. Alex lives with his other parent who is responsible for his daily care. While the claimant contributes financially, this alone does not satisfy the responsibility requirement for Child Benefit eligibility, which requires living with the child and being responsible for day-to-day care.

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and this would typically be the person Alex lives with who provides his day-to-day care.
